title: Children out of school (% of primary school age)
definition: Children out of school are the percentage of primary-school-age children who are not enrolled in primary or secondary school. Children in the official primary age group that are in preprimary education should be considered out of school.
unit: percent of primary school age population

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: Data API, UN Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), uri: https://databrowser.uis.unesco.org/resources, note: The data are obtained through the UIS API.  Detailed documentation is available at: https://api.uis.unesco.org/api/public/documentation/, publisher: UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS), type: Bulk file (csv), date accessed: 2026-03-19, date published: 2026-02

editorial.shortHook: A map of who is missing from primary school.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Burkina Faso, Suriname, Niger, while the lightest include Korea, Rep., Uruguay, Switzerland; that contrast is the fastest way to read the out-of-school primary age pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Out-of-school primary-age shares give players a direct education-access signal.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Primary enrollment can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Net primary enrollment lives in the same category, so it can feel plausible until the high and low countries are checked.
Secondary enrollment lives in the same category, so it can feel plausible until the high and low countries are checked.

editorial.difficultyReason: Standard difficulty: the map has readable regional structure, but the best answer still depends on checking the unit and country anchors.
regionalSignals: Education indicators can be shaped by enrollment systems and data coverage.
Compare youth-heavy countries with higher-income service economies.

reviewNotes: Batch 3 promotion: clear education-access concept with just enough coverage to pass the data gate.
Daily-worthy when paired against non-education distractors or broader enrollment maps, not secondary out-of-school rows.
